heading: 1.1 What is Apex?
text: {{c1::Strongly typed}}: All variables and expressions have a data type, checked at compile time. Invalid references fail fast.

heading: 1.1 What is Apex?
text: {{c1::On-demand}}: Runs entirely on Salesforce's multitenant Lightning Platform — written, compiled, and executed in the cloud.

heading: 1.1 What is Apex?
text: {{c1::Java-like syntax}}: Familiar for developers who know Java, JavaScript, C#, or similar languages.

heading: 1.1 What is Apex?
text: {{c1::Transaction control}}: Supports Savepoints and rollback — you can control the atomic unit of work.

heading: 1.1 What is Apex?
text: {{c1::Built-in support}}: DML, SOQL, SOSL, locking, custom public API calls, and metadata dependency tracking are all native.

heading: 1.2 The 8 Core Characteristics of Apex
text: {{c1::Integrated}}: DML, inline SOQL/SOSL, bulk processing, locking, public API calls, and dependency warnings are built in.

heading: 1.2 The 8 Core Characteristics of Apex
text: {{c1::Easy to use}}: Based on familiar Java idioms — variables, conditionals, loops, objects, arrays.

heading: 1.2 The 8 Core Characteristics of Apex
text: {{c1::Data focused}}: Threads multiple query and DML statements into one unit of work, like database stored procedures.

heading: 1.2 The 8 Core Characteristics of Apex
text: {{c1::Rigorous}}: Direct references to schema objects. Fails at compile time if references are invalid.

heading: 1.2 The 8 Core Characteristics of Apex
text: {{c1::Hosted}}: Interpreted, executed, and controlled entirely by the Lightning Platform.

heading: 1.2 The 8 Core Characteristics of Apex
text: {{c1::Multitenant aware}}: Runtime engine guards against runaway code to prevent monopolizing shared resources.

heading: 1.2 The 8 Core Characteristics of Apex
text: {{c1::Easy to test}}: Built-in support for unit test creation, execution, and code coverage.

heading: 1.2 The 8 Core Characteristics of Apex
text: {{c1::Versioned}}: You can save Apex against different API versions to maintain behavior over time.

heading: 1.3 When to Use Apex (vs Flow / LWC / Visualforce)
text: {{c1::Use Apex when}}: You need to create web services, email services, complex multi-object validation, complex business processes Flow can't handle, custom transactional logic, or logic that must run regardless of UI origin.

heading: 1.3 When to Use Apex (vs Flow / LWC / Visualforce)
text: {{c1::Use Lightning Web Components}}: For custom user interfaces in Lightning Experience, the Salesforce mobile app, or standalone apps. LWC follows W3C web standards.

heading: 1.3 When to Use Apex (vs Flow / LWC / Visualforce)
text: {{c1::Use Visualforce}}: For wizards, multi-step processes, custom flow control, or specialized data-driven UI patterns.

heading: 1.3 When to Use Apex (vs Flow / LWC / Visualforce)
text: {{c1::Use SOAP API}}: When you only process one type of record at a time and don't need transactional control.

heading: 1.4 What Apex Cannot Do
text: What Apex Cannot Do: {{c1::Render UI elements other than error messages.}}

heading: 1.4 What Apex Cannot Do
text: What Apex Cannot Do: {{c1::Change standard functionality — it can only prevent it or add to it.}}

heading: 1.4 What Apex Cannot Do
text: What Apex Cannot Do: {{c1::Create temporary files.}}

heading: 1.4 What Apex Cannot Do
text: What Apex Cannot Do: {{c1::Spawn threads.}}

heading: 1.4 What Apex Cannot Do
text: What Apex Cannot Do: {{c1::It is NOT a general-purpose programming language — it is tailored to Salesforce data access and manipulation.}}

heading: 1.5 The Apex Development Process
text: {{c1::1. Choose an org}}: Sandbox, scratch org, or Developer Edition. Never develop directly in production.

heading: 1.5 The Apex Development Process
text: {{c1::2. Learn Apex}}: Use Trailhead, Apex Developer Center, and this study guide.

heading: 1.5 The Apex Development Process
text: {{c1::3. Write Apex}}: Use VS Code + Salesforce Extensions, Code Builder, or Developer Console.

heading: 1.5 The Apex Development Process
text: {{c1::4. Write tests}}: Use test-driven development — write tests at the same time as code.

heading: 1.5 The Apex Development Process
text: {{c1::5. Deploy to sandbox}}: Run final unit tests in an isolated environment.

heading: 1.5 The Apex Development Process
text: {{c1::6. Deploy to production}}: Via change sets, Salesforce CLI, or Metadata API.

heading: 1.6 Choosing a Salesforce Org for Development
text: {{c1::Sandbox (Recommended)}}: Copy of production metadata, isolated. Developer and Developer Pro sandboxes support source tracking, Salesforce CLI, Code Builder, and DevOps Center.

heading: 1.6 Choosing a Salesforce Org for Development
text: {{c1::Scratch orgs (Recommended)}}: Source-driven, temporary, fully configurable deployment. Max 30-day lifespan, default 7 days. Great for emulating different editions.

heading: 1.6 Choosing a Salesforce Org for Development
text: {{c1::Developer Edition}}: Free org with Enterprise Edition features. Can become outdated and expire. No source tracking, can't be used in DevOps Center.

heading: 1.6 Choosing a Salesforce Org for Development
text: {{c1::Trial Edition}}: 30-day expiration. Good for evaluation. Triggers are disabled when you convert to another edition.

heading: 1.6 Choosing a Salesforce Org for Development
text: {{c1::Production (Not supported)}}: Never develop directly in production — live users can destabilize data or corrupt the application.

heading: 1.7 Development Environments
text: {{c1::VS Code + Extensions}}: The primary modern environment. Supports scratch orgs, sandboxes, and DE orgs. Works with Apex, LWC, and Visualforce.

heading: 1.7 Development Environments
text: {{c1::Code Builder}}: Browser-based VS Code with everything pre-installed — work from any computer.

heading: 1.7 Development Environments
text: {{c1::Developer Console}}: Built-in IDE in Salesforce. Supports writing, compiling, debugging, testing, SOQL queries, and code coverage inspection.

heading: 1.7 Development Environments
text: {{c1::Setup Code Editors}}: View and edit Apex classes and triggers from Salesforce Setup. Auto-compile on save. Not available in production orgs.

heading: 1.7 Development Environments
text: {{c1::Agentforce for Developers}}: AI-powered code generation from natural language prompts, inline auto-complete, and test case generation. Available in VS Code.

heading: 2.1 All Apex Data Types
text: {{c1::Primitives}}: Integer, Double, Long, Decimal, Boolean, String, ID, Date, Datetime, Time, Blob, Object.

heading: 2.1 All Apex Data Types
text: {{c1::sObjects}}: Generic sObject or specific types like Account, Contact, MyCustomObject__c.

heading: 2.1 All Apex Data Types
text: {{c1::Collections}}: Lists, Sets, and Maps of primitives, sObjects, or user-defined types.

heading: 2.1 All Apex Data Types
text: {{c1::Enums}}: A typed list of identifiers for values that don't have a numerical order.

heading: 2.1 All Apex Data Types
text: {{c1::User-defined types}}: Objects created from your own Apex classes.

heading: 2.1 All Apex Data Types
text: {{c1::System-supplied types}}: Objects from Salesforce-provided classes like Database, UserInfo, Schema.

heading: 2.1 All Apex Data Types
text: {{c1::Null}}: Can be assigned to any variable. All Apex variables are initialized to null by default.

heading: 2.2 Primitive Data Types
text: {{c1::Blob}}: Binary data stored as a single object. Use toString() and valueOf() to convert. Used for document bodies, attachments, and crypto.

heading: 2.2 Primitive Data Types
text: {{c1::Boolean}}: true, false, or null. Example: Boolean isWinner = true;

heading: 2.2 Primitive Data Types
text: {{c1::Date}}: A particular day with no time. Always create via system static method. Only Integer addition/subtraction works on Date.

heading: 2.2 Primitive Data Types
text: {{c1::Datetime}}: A day and time (timestamp). Use system static methods. Supports Integer and Double addition/subtraction.

heading: 2.2 Primitive Data Types
text: {{c1::Decimal}}: Arbitrary precision number. Currency fields are Decimal. Use setScale() to control decimal places. Two numerically-equal Decimals with different scale (1.1 vs 1.10) have different hashcodes.

heading: 2.2 Primitive Data Types
text: {{c1::Double}}: 64-bit number with decimal point. Range is -2^63 to 2^63-1. Scientific notation (e) not supported.

heading: 2.2 Primitive Data Types
text: {{c1::ID}}: Any valid 18-character Salesforce record ID. Auto-converts 15-char to 18-char. Invalid IDs throw runtime exception.

heading: 2.2 Primitive Data Types
text: {{c1::Integer}}: 32-bit number without decimal. Range: -2,147,483,648 to 2,147,483,647.

heading: 2.2 Primitive Data Types
text: {{c1::Long}}: 64-bit number without decimal. Range: -2^63 to 2^63-1. Use when wider range than Integer is needed. Append L: 2147483648L.

heading: 2.2 Primitive Data Types
text: {{c1::Object}}: Any supported Apex data type. All types inherit from Object. Cast to a specific type to access its members.

heading: 2.2 Primitive Data Types
text: {{c1::String}}: Any set of characters in single quotes. Supports ==, !=, <, <=, >, >=. Comparison is NOT case-sensitive and uses the context user's locale.

heading: 2.2 Primitive Data Types
text: {{c1::Time}}: A particular time. Always create via system static method.

heading: 2.3 String Escapes and Multiline Strings
text: {{c1::Escape sequences}}: \b (backspace), \t (tab), \n (line feed), \f (form feed), \r (carriage return), \s (space), \" (double quote), \' (single quote), \\ (backslash).

heading: 2.3 String Escapes and Multiline Strings
text: {{c1::sObject String rules}}: sObject String fields can never be empty (only null) and cannot have leading/trailing whitespace — required for database storage.

heading: 2.3 String Escapes and Multiline Strings
text: {{c1::Apex String rules}}: Apex Strings CAN be null, empty, or have leading/trailing whitespace — useful for building messages.

heading: 2.3 String Escapes and Multiline Strings
text: {{c1::Multiline string}}: Starts with three single quotes (''') followed by a newline. Ends with three single quotes. Leading whitespace is stripped, trailing whitespace is stripped unless you use \s.

heading: 2.3 String Escapes and Multiline Strings
text: {{c1::Multiline concatenation}}: Use \ at the end of a line to concatenate lines without inserting newlines.

heading: 2.4 Lists
text: {{c1::Create empty list}}: Use the new keyword and List keyword with the element type in <>.

heading: 2.4 Lists
text: {{c1::Add elements}}: Use the .add() method, or use literal notation with braces.

heading: 2.4 Lists
text: {{c1::Get / Set}}: Use .get(index) and .set(index, value). Square brackets also work: colors[0] = 'Green'.

heading: 2.4 Lists
text: {{c1::Array notation}}: For one-dimensional lists, you can use Java-style array notation: String[] colors = new String[3];

heading: 2.4 Lists
text: {{c1::Nested lists}}: Up to 7 levels of nested collections (8 levels overall). Example: List<List<Set<Integer>>>.

heading: 2.4 Lists
text: {{c1::List.sort()}}: Sorts in ascending order for primitives. For custom types, implement Comparable. For SelectOption, sorts by value first, then label.

heading: 2.4 Lists
text: {{c1::Elastic vs array}}: When you add via .add(), the list grows as needed. When you assign via square brackets, the list behaves like a fixed-size array.

heading: 2.5 Sets
text: {{c1::No duplicates}}: Adding an existing element has no effect. Highly optimized for .contains() checks.

heading: 2.5 Sets
text: {{c1::Unordered}}: You cannot access a set element by index. You can only iterate.

heading: 2.5 Sets
text: {{c1::Hash structure}}: Unlike Java, Apex uses a hash structure for all sets. You don't specify HashSet vs TreeSet.

heading: 2.5 Sets
text: {{c1::Create with literal}}: Set<String> s = new Set<String>{'a', 'b', 'c'};

heading: 2.5 Sets
text: {{c1::Core methods}}: .add(value), .contains(value), .remove(value), .size(), .clear().

heading: 2.6 Maps
text: {{c1::Keys}}: Can be any primitive data type. Each key must be unique.

heading: 2.6 Maps
text: {{c1::Values}}: Can be any data type — primitives, sObjects, collections. Values can be duplicated.

heading: 2.6 Maps
text: {{c1::Create with literal}}: Use => to separate key and value pairs.

heading: 2.6 Maps
text: {{c1::Core methods}}: .put(key, value), .get(key), .containsKey(key), .keySet(), .values(), .remove(key), .size().

heading: 2.7 Enums
text: {{c1::Purpose}}: An abstract data type whose values each take on exactly one of a finite set of identifiers you specify.

heading: 2.7 Enums
text: {{c1::When to use}}: For sets of possible values that don't have a numerical order — e.g., seasons, card suits, order statuses.

heading: 2.7 Enums
text: {{c1::Switch-friendly}}: Enums work especially well with switch statements in Apex.

heading: 2.8 Variables, Constants, and Assignment
text: {{c1::Declaration}}: datatype variable_name [= value]; — always end statements with a semicolon.

heading: 2.8 Variables, Constants, and Assignment
text: {{c1::Pass-by-value (primitives)}}: Integer, String, etc. are passed into methods by value. Changes inside the method are lost when the method returns.

heading: 2.8 Variables, Constants, and Assignment
text: {{c1::Pass-by-reference (sObjects)}}: sObjects are passed by reference. Changes to field values persist after the method returns.

heading: 2.8 Variables, Constants, and Assignment
text: {{c1::Constants}}: Use the final keyword. Once initialized, the value cannot change.

heading: 2.8 Variables, Constants, and Assignment
text: {{c1::Assignment operators}}: Valid: =, +=, -=, *=, /=, |=, &=, ++, --.

heading: 2.9 Operators and Expressions
text: {{c1::Safe navigation ?.}}: Returns null instead of throwing NullPointerException when navigating a chain. Example: account?.Contact?.Email.

heading: 2.9 Operators and Expressions
text: {{c1::Null coalescing ??}}: Returns the right-hand operand when the left-hand is null. Replaces verbose null checks.

heading: 2.9 Operators and Expressions
text: {{c1::String comparison}}: Apex Strings support ==, !=, <, <=, >, >=. NOT case-sensitive, uses context user's locale.

heading: 2.9 Operators and Expressions
text: {{c1::Ternary}}: boolean ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse

heading: 2.9 Operators and Expressions
text: {{c1::instanceof}}: Check if an sObject is a specific type: if (sobj instanceof Account) { ... }

heading: 2.10 Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom)
text: Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom): {{c1::1. Grouping and prefix: {} () ++ --}}

heading: 2.10 Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom)
text: Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom): {{c1::2. Unary, type cast, new: ~ ! -x +x (type) new}}

heading: 2.10 Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom)
text: Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom): {{c1::3. Multiplication/division: * /}}

heading: 2.10 Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom)
text: Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom): {{c1::4. Addition/subtraction: + -}}

heading: 2.10 Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom)
text: Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom): {{c1::5. Shift: << >> >>>}}

heading: 2.10 Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom)
text: Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom): {{c1::6. Comparison: < <= > >= instanceof}}

heading: 2.10 Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom)
text: Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom): {{c1::7. Equality: == !=}}

heading: 2.10 Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom)
text: Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom): {{c1::8-10. Bitwise: & ^ |}}

heading: 2.10 Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom)
text: Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom): {{c1::11-12. Logical: && ||}}

heading: 2.10 Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom)
text: Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom): {{c1::13. Null coalescing: ??}}

heading: 2.10 Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom)
text: Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom): {{c1::14. Ternary: ?:}}

heading: 2.10 Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom)
text: Operator Precedence (Top to Bottom): {{c1::15. Assignment: = += -= *= /= &= <<= >>= >>>=}}

heading: 2.11 Rules of Conversion
text: {{c1::Implicit numeric promotion}}: Hierarchy: Integer → Long → Double → Decimal. Lower types can be assigned to higher types without explicit conversion.

heading: 2.11 Rules of Conversion
text: {{c1::ID ↔ String}}: IDs can be assigned to Strings. Strings can be assigned to IDs but throw runtime exception if invalid.

heading: 2.11 Rules of Conversion
text: {{c1::Date → Datetime}}: Both implicit and explicit casting work. Time component is zeroed out.

heading: 2.11 Rules of Conversion
text: {{c1::Overflow warning}}: Apex does NOT throw an exception for overflow/underflow — Integer i = 2147483647 + 1; yields -2147483648. Use Long (append L) for larger computations.

heading: 2.11 Rules of Conversion
text: {{c1::Integer division}}: 5/3 returns 1 (Integer) before implicit conversion. Use 5.0/3.0 to preserve fractions.

heading: 3.1 If-Else Statements
text: {{c1::Basic syntax}}: Works just like Java. The else is always optional and groups with the closest if.

heading: 3.1 If-Else Statements
text: {{c1::else if chains}}: Chain multiple conditions. else is always optional.

heading: 3.1 If-Else Statements
text: {{c1::Braces}}: Curly braces are required for multi-statement blocks, optional for single-statement blocks.

heading: 3.2 Switch Statements (Modern Apex)
text: {{c1::Syntax}}: switch on expression { when value { ... } when else { ... } }

heading: 3.2 Switch Statements (Modern Apex)
text: {{c1::No fall-through}}: After a when block executes, the switch exits. This is different from C/Java switch.

heading: 3.2 Switch Statements (Modern Apex)
text: {{c1::Allowed expression types}}: Integer, Long, sObject, String, Enum.

heading: 3.2 Switch Statements (Modern Apex)
text: {{c1::Multiple values}}: A when clause can have multiple comma-separated literals: when 2, 3, 4 { ... }

heading: 3.2 Switch Statements (Modern Apex)
text: {{c1::sObject type matching}}: switch on sobject { when Account a { ... } when Contact c { ... } } — implicit instanceof + cast.

heading: 3.2 Switch Statements (Modern Apex)
text: {{c1::when else}}: Optional default block. Salesforce recommends including it, especially with enums (in case a managed package adds new values).

heading: 3.3 The Five Loop Types
text: {{c1::do-while}}: Runs the body first, then checks the condition. Always executes at least once. Curly braces are required.

heading: 3.3 The Five Loop Types
text: {{c1::while}}: Checks the condition before the body. May execute zero times.

heading: 3.3 The Five Loop Types
text: {{c1::for (classic)}}: Three-part: initialization; exit condition; increment.

heading: 3.3 The Five Loop Types
text: {{c1::for (collection)}}: Iterates a list or set. Enhanced for-each loop.

heading: 3.3 The Five Loop Types
text: {{c1::for (SOQL)}}: Iterates query results directly. Best for bulk processing — heap-efficient.

heading: 3.4 Loop Control
text: {{c1::break}}: Exits the entire loop immediately.

heading: 3.4 Loop Control
text: {{c1::continue}}: Skips the rest of the current iteration and proceeds to the next.

heading: 4.1 Defining a Class
text: {{c1::Basic syntax}}: public class MyClass { ... }

heading: 4.1 Defining a Class
text: {{c1::Default access}}: If you omit a modifier, the class is private (only visible within the same namespace).

heading: 4.2 Class Modifiers
text: {{c1::public}}: Visible to any Apex in the application or namespace.

heading: 4.2 Class Modifiers
text: {{c1::private (default)}}: Only visible within the same class. Not accessible from other classes.

heading: 4.2 Class Modifiers
text: {{c1::global}}: Visible to all Apex code everywhere, including code in other namespaces. Required for exposed web services and managed package APIs.

heading: 4.2 Class Modifiers
text: {{c1::abstract}}: Class cannot be instantiated. Used as a base class. Can contain abstract methods (no body).

heading: 4.2 Class Modifiers
text: {{c1::virtual}}: Class can be extended. Use virtual when subclasses need to override methods.

heading: 4.2 Class Modifiers
text: {{c1::with sharing}}: Enforces the running user's sharing rules and record-level access.

heading: 4.2 Class Modifiers
text: {{c1::without sharing}}: Bypasses sharing rules. Use carefully — typically for system-level utilities.

heading: 4.2 Class Modifiers
text: {{c1::inherited sharing}}: Inherits sharing context from the calling code. Available in API v48.0+.

heading: 4.3 Static vs Instance
text: {{c1::Static}}: Belongs to the class, not an instance. Accessed via ClassName.method(). Only one copy exists.

heading: 4.3 Static vs Instance
text: {{c1::Instance}}: Belongs to an object. Requires an instance to call. Each object has its own copy.

heading: 4.3 Static vs Instance
text: {{c1::Static initialization}}: Static variables are initialized when the class is first loaded. Use static { } block for static initialization code.

heading: 4.3 Static vs Instance
text: {{c1::Static methods}}: Cannot access instance variables or methods. Often used for utility/helper methods.

heading: 4.4 Methods
text: {{c1::Access modifiers}}: public, private, protected, global — control visibility.

heading: 4.4 Methods
text: {{c1::Static vs instance}}: Static methods belong to the class; instance methods require an object.

heading: 4.4 Methods
text: {{c1::Return type}}: Specify a return type (Integer, String, void, sObject, etc.) or void for no return.

heading: 4.4 Methods
text: {{c1::Overloading}}: Apex supports method overloading — multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists.

heading: 4.4 Methods
text: {{c1::Pass-by-value vs reference}}: Primitives pass by value. sObjects pass by reference (field changes persist).

heading: 4.5 Constructors
text: {{c1::Purpose}}: Initialize an object when it is created via new.

heading: 4.5 Constructors
text: {{c1::Default constructor}}: If you don't write a constructor, Apex provides a no-argument constructor automatically.

heading: 4.5 Constructors
text: {{c1::Overloaded constructors}}: You can write multiple constructors with different parameter lists.

heading: 4.5 Constructors
text: {{c1::No return type}}: Constructors don't have a return type, not even void.

heading: 4.6 Interfaces
text: {{c1::Purpose}}: Define a contract — methods that implementing classes must provide. Apex supports interfaces and abstract classes.

heading: 4.6 Interfaces
text: {{c1::Method signatures}}: Interface methods have no body. Implementing class must provide the body.

heading: 4.6 Interfaces
text: {{c1::Multiple interfaces}}: A class can implement multiple interfaces (use commas), but can only extend one class.

heading: 4.6 Interfaces
text: {{c1::Common interfaces}}: Database.Batchable<SObject>, Schedulable, Queueable, Comparable, Iterator, Iterable.

heading: 4.7 Properties
text: {{c1::What is a property?}}: A property looks like a field but is actually a pair of get/set accessors. Cleaner than explicit getter/setter methods.

heading: 4.7 Properties
text: {{c1::Auto-properties}}: public Integer Count { get; set; } — Apex auto-generates the backing field and accessors.

heading: 4.7 Properties
text: {{c1::Custom get/set}}: You can provide custom getter and setter logic blocks.

heading: 4.8 Inheritance and Polymorphism
text: {{c1::extends}}: A class can extend exactly one parent class. Use 'virtual' on the parent to allow overriding.

heading: 4.8 Inheritance and Polymorphism
text: {{c1::override}}: Use the override keyword to override a virtual or abstract method from a parent.

heading: 4.8 Inheritance and Polymorphism
text: {{c1::virtual}}: Methods marked virtual can be overridden. Abstract methods MUST be overridden.

heading: 4.8 Inheritance and Polymorphism
text: {{c1::super}}: Call the parent implementation with super.methodName().

heading: 5.1 sObject Basics
text: {{c1::What is an sObject?}}: Any data type that represents a Salesforce record — standard (Account, Contact) or custom (MyObject__c).

heading: 5.1 sObject Basics
text: {{c1::Generic sObject}}: sObject generic type can hold any record. Use specific types (Account, Contact) whenever possible for type safety.

heading: 5.1 sObject Basics
text: {{c1::Custom objects}}: Always end with __c. Custom fields end with __c.

heading: 5.1 sObject Basics
text: {{c1::Creating records}}: Use the new keyword and the sObject type name. Pass field values as named parameters.

heading: 5.2 DML Statements
text: {{c1::insert}}: Adds new records. Sets Id automatically.

heading: 5.2 DML Statements
text: {{c1::update}}: Modifies existing records. Must have Id populated.

heading: 5.2 DML Statements
text: {{c1::upsert}}: Updates if record with matching external/Id field exists; otherwise inserts. Requires an external ID field or Id.

heading: 5.2 DML Statements
text: {{c1::delete}}: Removes records. Goes to Recycle Bin for 15 days; can be undeleted within that window.

heading: 5.2 DML Statements
text: {{c1::undelete}}: Restores records from the Recycle Bin.

heading: 5.2 DML Statements
text: {{c1::merge}}: Combines up to 3 records of the same sObject type into one. Merges child records too.

heading: 5.3 DML Syntax — Two Forms
text: {{c1::Statement form}}: Direct DML — runs 'all or nothing'. If one record fails, the entire transaction rolls back.

heading: 5.3 DML Syntax — Two Forms
text: {{c1::Database class form}}: Database.insert(records, allOrNone). Use false to allow partial success.

heading: 5.3 DML Syntax — Two Forms
text: {{c1::When to use Database class}}: When you need partial-success behavior or to inspect individual record errors. Returns Database.SaveResult[] or Database.DeleteResult[].

heading: 5.4 Savepoints and Rollback
text: {{c1::Savepoint}}: Marks a point in the transaction you can roll back to.

heading: 5.4 Savepoints and Rollback
text: {{c1::Limits}}: Only 10 active savepoints per request. Each rollback decrements the count.

heading: 5.4 Savepoints and Rollback
text: {{c1::Use case}}: Try a DML operation, roll back if it fails, and continue with logic.

heading: 5.5 Locking Records (FOR UPDATE)
text: {{c1::FOR UPDATE}}: Locks sObject records during the transaction to prevent race conditions.

heading: 5.5 Locking Records (FOR UPDATE)
text: {{c1::When to use}}: When subsequent code will update the records and you need to prevent other transactions from modifying them.

heading: 5.6 Field Relationships
text: {{c1::Parent-to-child}}: Traverse a lookup using dot notation: account.Owner.Name.

heading: 5.6 Field Relationships
text: {{c1::Child-to-parent}}: Query through a relationship: SELECT Account.Owner.Name FROM Contact.

heading: 5.6 Field Relationships
text: {{c1::Custom relationship}}: For custom __r: SELECT My_Custom_Child__r.Name FROM Account.

heading: 6.1 SOQL Basics
text: {{c1::What is SOQL?}}: Salesforce Object Query Language. Query relational data from a single object or related objects.

heading: 6.1 SOQL Basics
text: {{c1::Basic syntax}}: SELECT field1, field2 FROM ObjectName WHERE condition

heading: 6.1 SOQL Basics
text: {{c1::Query all fields}}: Don't use SELECT * — always list fields explicitly for performance and clarity.

heading: 6.1 SOQL Basics
text: {{c1::Child-to-parent}}: SELECT Id, Account.Name FROM Contact

heading: 6.1 SOQL Basics
text: {{c1::Parent-to-child}}: SELECT Id, (SELECT Id, LastName FROM Contacts) FROM Account

heading: 6.2 SOQL Clauses
text: {{c1::WHERE}}: Filter records. Use bind variables for user input: WHERE Name = :varName

heading: 6.2 SOQL Clauses
text: {{c1::ORDER BY}}: Sort results: ORDER BY Name ASC, CreatedDate DESC

heading: 6.2 SOQL Clauses
text: {{c1::LIMIT}}: Cap returned records: LIMIT 100

heading: 6.2 SOQL Clauses
text: {{c1::OFFSET}}: Skip records for pagination: OFFSET 200

heading: 6.2 SOQL Clauses
text: {{c1::GROUP BY / HAVING}}: Aggregate. SELECT COUNT(Id), Industry FROM Account GROUP BY Industry HAVING COUNT(Id) > 5

heading: 6.2 SOQL Clauses
text: {{c1::FOR UPDATE}}: Lock records: SELECT Id FROM Account WHERE Name = 'Acme' FOR UPDATE

heading: 6.2 SOQL Clauses
text: {{c1::FOR REFERENCE}}: Used in trigger context variables to get old values without locking.

heading: 6.2 SOQL Clauses
text: {{c1::UPDATE TRACKING / VIEWSTAT}}: Enable tracking for change data capture events.

heading: 6.3 SOQL with SECURITY_ENFORCED / WITH USER_MODE
text: {{c1::WITH USER_MODE}}: Enforces field-level security (FLS) and sharing rules. Recommended over WITH SECURITY_ENFORCED.

heading: 6.3 SOQL with SECURITY_ENFORCED / WITH USER_MODE
text: {{c1::WITH SYSTEM_MODE}}: Bypasses FLS and sharing. Use for system code only (e.g., setup operations).

heading: 6.3 SOQL with SECURITY_ENFORCED / WITH USER_MODE
text: {{c1::WITHOUT sharing context}}: Use UserMode/AccessLevel enums: WITH USER_MODE or [SELECT Id FROM Account AS USER] or [AS SYSTEM].

heading: 6.4 Aggregate Functions
text: {{c1::COUNT()}}: Returns Integer. SELECT COUNT(Id) FROM Account

heading: 6.4 Aggregate Functions
text: {{c1::SUM(), AVG(), MIN(), MAX()}}: Numeric aggregates. SELECT MAX(Amount), MIN(Amount) FROM Opportunity

heading: 6.4 Aggregate Functions
text: {{c1::GROUP BY}}: Required for most aggregates. SELECT Industry, COUNT(Id) cnt FROM Account GROUP BY Industry

heading: 6.5 Relationship Queries
text: {{c1::Child relationship name}}: Find the relationship name via object manager. For Contacts on Account, it's 'Contacts' (plural).

heading: 6.5 Relationship Queries
text: {{c1::Custom relationship}}: Ends with __r for the parent reference. SELECT My_Child__c FROM Parent__c

heading: 6.5 Relationship Queries
text: {{c1::Filter on related fields}}: WHERE Account.Industry = 'Tech' — filter using a related object's field.

heading: 6.6 SOSL Basics
text: {{c1::What is SOSL?}}: Salesforce Object Search Language. Free-text search across multiple, unrelated objects at once.

heading: 6.6 SOSL Basics
text: {{c1::Syntax}}: FIND 'searchTerm' IN ALL FIELDS RETURNING Object(field1, field2)

heading: 6.6 SOSL Basics
text: {{c1::Return type}}: List<List<SObject>> — a list of result lists, one per object type.

heading: 6.6 SOSL Basics
text: {{c1::When to use}}: When you don't know which object has the data, or need to search many objects at once. Faster for free-text than SOQL on large text fields.

heading: 6.7 Dynamic SOQL and SOSL
text: {{c1::Database.query()}}: Build SOQL as a String at runtime. Use bind variables to prevent SOQL injection.

heading: 6.7 Dynamic SOQL and SOSL
text: {{c1::Search.query()}}: Same idea for SOSL. Returns List<List<SObject>>.

heading: 6.7 Dynamic SOQL and SOSL
text: {{c1::Security}}: Use String.escapeSingleQuotes() and Database.query with bind variables to prevent injection.

heading: 7.1 Trigger Syntax
text: {{c1::Definition}}: trigger TriggerName on ObjectName (events) { ... }

heading: 7.1 Trigger Syntax
text: {{c1::Events}}: before insert, before update, before delete, after insert, after update, after delete, after undelete.

heading: 7.1 Trigger Syntax
text: {{c1::Combine events}}: A single trigger can handle multiple events. Branch with Trigger.operationType.

heading: 7.2 Trigger Context Variables
text: {{c1::Trigger.new}}: List of new sObject versions. Available in all events. Read-only in after triggers.

heading: 7.2 Trigger Context Variables
text: {{c1::Trigger.old}}: List of old sObject versions. Available in update, delete, undelete.

heading: 7.2 Trigger Context Variables
text: {{c1::Trigger.newMap}}: Map<Id, sObject> of new versions, keyed by Id. Available in update, insert, delete, undelete (post-save).

heading: 7.2 Trigger Context Variables
text: {{c1::Trigger.oldMap}}: Map<Id, sObject> of old versions, keyed by Id. Available in update, delete.

heading: 7.2 Trigger Context Variables
text: {{c1::Trigger.isInsert}}: Boolean. True if the trigger fired on insert.

heading: 7.2 Trigger Context Variables
text: {{c1::Trigger.isUpdate / isDelete / isUndelete / isBefore / isAfter}}: Booleans for the current context.

heading: 7.2 Trigger Context Variables
text: {{c1::Trigger.size}}: Number of records in the trigger invocation — useful for bulk handling.

heading: 7.2 Trigger Context Variables
text: {{c1::Trigger.operationType}}: Enum: BEFORE_INSERT, BEFORE_UPDATE, BEFORE_DELETE, AFTER_INSERT, AFTER_UPDATE, AFTER_DELETE, AFTER_UNDELETE.

heading: 7.3 Trigger Events and What They Allow
text: {{c1::Before insert / before update}}: Modify field values on Trigger.new directly. No DML needed. Use for validation and data normalization.

heading: 7.3 Trigger Events and What They Allow
text: {{c1::After insert / after update}}: Trigger.new is read-only. Use for related record creation and other side effects. DML allowed.

heading: 7.3 Trigger Events and What They Allow
text: {{c1::Before delete}}: Use Trigger.old to inspect. Can call addError() to block deletion.

heading: 7.3 Trigger Events and What They Allow
text: {{c1::After delete / undelete}}: Use Trigger.old or Trigger.new. Good for cleanup or restoration logic.

heading: 7.4 Trigger Best Practices
text: {{c1::Bulkify}}: Never put SOQL or DML inside a for loop. Collect criteria into Sets/Lists, query once, iterate results.

heading: 7.4 Trigger Best Practices
text: {{c1::One trigger per object}}: Use a single trigger per object with a handler class to keep logic organized.

heading: 7.4 Trigger Best Practices
text: {{c1::Avoid recursion}}: Use a static Boolean flag or Set<Id> to prevent recursive firing from cascading updates.

heading: 7.4 Trigger Best Practices
text: {{c1::Logic-less triggers}}: Best practice: put logic in a handler class, keep the trigger a one-liner that calls it.

heading: 8.1 Per-Transaction Limits (Synchronous)
text: {{c1::SOQL queries}}: 100 queries per synchronous transaction. 200 in async (Batch/Future/Queueable/Schedulable).

heading: 8.1 Per-Transaction Limits (Synchronous)
text: {{c1::SOQL query rows}}: 50,000 rows retrieved in a single transaction.

heading: 8.1 Per-Transaction Limits (Synchronous)
text: {{c1::DML statements}}: 150 statements per synchronous transaction.

heading: 8.1 Per-Transaction Limits (Synchronous)
text: {{c1::DML rows}}: 10,000 records processed per transaction.

heading: 8.1 Per-Transaction Limits (Synchronous)
text: {{c1::Heap size}}: 6 MB synchronous, 12 MB asynchronous.

heading: 8.1 Per-Transaction Limits (Synchronous)
text: {{c1::CPU time}}: 10,000 ms synchronous, 60,000 ms asynchronous.

heading: 8.1 Per-Transaction Limits (Synchronous)
text: {{c1::Callouts}}: 100 HTTP callouts per transaction.

heading: 8.1 Per-Transaction Limits (Synchronous)
text: {{c1::Email}}: 100 sendEmail methods per transaction.

heading: 8.1 Per-Transaction Limits (Synchronous)
text: {{c1::Future methods}}: 50 per transaction.

heading: 8.1 Per-Transaction Limits (Synchronous)
text: {{c1::Queueable jobs}}: 1 per transaction (50 with TransactionFinalizers in chain).

heading: 8.2 Bulkification
text: {{c1::What it means}}: Code that processes a List of 200 records with the same number of SOQL/DML as 1 record.

heading: 8.2 Bulkification
text: {{c1::Bad pattern}}: for (Account a : Trigger.new) { List<Contact> cs = [SELECT Id FROM Contact WHERE AccountId = :a.Id]; } — hits SOQL limit fast.

heading: 8.2 Bulkification
text: {{c1::Good pattern}}: Collect all AccountIds into a Set, run ONE SOQL, iterate.

heading: 8.2 Bulkification
text: {{c1::DML at the end}}: Build lists of records to update/insert, then perform ONE DML on the whole list at the end of the trigger.

heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
text: The Order of Execution: {{c1::1. System validation rules (required fields, field format)}}

heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
text: The Order of Execution: {{c1::2. Before triggers run}}

heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
text: The Order of Execution: {{c1::3. Custom validation rules}}

heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
text: The Order of Execution: {{c1::4. Record saved to database (but not committed)}}

heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
text: The Order of Execution: {{c1::5. After triggers run}}

heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
text: The Order of Execution: {{c1::6. Assignment rules}}

heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
text: The Order of Execution: {{c1::7. Auto-response rules}}

heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
text: The Order of Execution: {{c1::8. Workflow rules}}

heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
text: The Order of Execution: {{c1::9. Processes (Flows, Process Builder — being retired)}}

heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
text: The Order of Execution: {{c1::10. Escalation rules}}

heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
text: The Order of Execution: {{c1::11. Entitlement rules}}

heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
text: The Order of Execution: {{c1::12. Roll-up summary fields update child records}}

heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
text: The Order of Execution: {{c1::13. Criteria-based sharing evaluation}}

heading: 8.3 The Order of Execution
text: The Order of Execution: {{c1::14. DML commits to database}}

heading: 8.4 Why Limits Matter
text: Why Limits Matter: {{c1::All Apex code runs on shared, multitenant infrastructure.}}

heading: 8.4 Why Limits Matter
text: Why Limits Matter: {{c1::Limits prevent one tenant's runaway code from degrading the platform for everyone.}}

heading: 8.4 Why Limits Matter
text: Why Limits Matter: {{c1::All Apex requests return 1 to 50,000 records — you must design for bulk.}}

heading: 8.4 Why Limits Matter
text: Why Limits Matter: {{c1::If you don't, you'll hit governor limits in production with real data.}}

heading: 9.1 Try-Catch-Finally
text: {{c1::Syntax}}: try { ... } catch (ExceptionType e) { ... } finally { ... }

heading: 9.1 Try-Catch-Finally
text: {{c1::finally block}}: Always runs, even if an exception is thrown. Use for cleanup (Database.rollback, logging).

heading: 9.2 Common Built-in Exceptions
text: {{c1::DmlException}}: Thrown when a DML operation fails. Use getDmlMessage(i) and getDmlId(i) to inspect each error.

heading: 9.2 Common Built-in Exceptions
text: {{c1::QueryException}}: Thrown when a SOQL query fails — invalid SOQL, query assigned to single sObject but returns 0 or many rows.

heading: 9.2 Common Built-in Exceptions
text: {{c1::NullPointerException}}: Thrown when dereferencing a null variable. Use safe navigation (?. ) to avoid.

heading: 9.2 Common Built-in Exceptions
text: {{c1::ListException}}: Thrown on invalid list operations — index out of bounds, modifying list during for-each.

heading: 9.2 Common Built-in Exceptions
text: {{c1::LimitException}}: Thrown when a governor limit is exceeded.

heading: 9.2 Common Built-in Exceptions
text: {{c1::MathException}}: Thrown on arithmetic errors like division by zero.

heading: 9.3 Custom Exceptions
text: {{c1::Define}}: Extend the Exception class. Optionally pass a message to the constructor.

heading: 9.3 Custom Exceptions
text: {{c1::Throw}}: throw new MyException('Something went wrong');

heading: 9.3 Custom Exceptions
text: {{c1::Catch}}: catch (MyException e) { System.debug(e.getMessage()); }

heading: 9.4 addError() vs Exceptions
text: {{c1::addError() in triggers}}: In a trigger, call e.addError('msg') to roll back the transaction and display the error to the user.

heading: 9.4 addError() vs Exceptions
text: {{c1::addError() on fields}}: acc.Name.addError('Name is required'); — error attached to a specific field.

heading: 9.4 addError() vs Exceptions
text: {{c1::When to use what}}: Use addError() in triggers for user-facing validation. Use try-catch for programmatic error handling.

heading: 10.1 Test Class Basics
text: {{c1::@IsTest annotation}}: Mark the class and methods as test code. @IsTest classes don't count against the 6 MB Apex code limit.

heading: 10.1 Test Class Basics
text: {{c1::Static test methods}}: Test methods must be static, return void, and take no arguments.

heading: 10.1 Test Class Basics
text: {{c1::testMethod keyword}}: Equivalent to @IsTest. Salesforce recommends @IsTest — testMethod may be versioned out.

heading: 10.1 Test Class Basics
text: {{c1::Required coverage}}: At least 75% of Apex code must be covered by tests. All tests must pass. Every trigger must have some coverage.

heading: 10.2 The @TestSetup Pattern
text: {{c1::Purpose}}: Create test data once for the entire class. Re-runs before each test method to ensure isolation.

heading: 10.2 The @TestSetup Pattern
text: {{c1::Usage}}: Annotate a method with @TestSetup. It must be static.

heading: 10.2 The @TestSetup Pattern
text: {{c1::Access in tests}}: Use [SELECT Id FROM Account LIMIT 1] to fetch the records created by @TestSetup.

heading: 10.3 Test.startTest / Test.stopTest
text: {{c1::Why}}: Apex sets a new governor limit boundary inside startTest/stopTest. Use to simulate large data and isolate async code.

heading: 10.3 Test.startTest / Test.stopTest
text: {{c1::Pattern}}: Wrap the code under test between startTest and stopTest. After stopTest, async code has completed.

heading: 10.3 Test.startTest / Test.stopTest
text: {{c1::Required for async}}: Future methods, Batch Apex, and Queueable jobs only execute inside a startTest/stopTest block.

heading: 10.4 Assertions
text: {{c1::System.assert}}: assert(condition) — fails if false.

heading: 10.4 Assertions
text: {{c1::System.assertEquals}}: assertEquals(expected, actual) — fails if not equal.

heading: 10.4 Assertions
text: {{c1::System.assertNotEquals}}: assertNotEquals(unexpected, actual)

heading: 10.4 Assertions
text: {{c1::Assert.areEqual}}: Assert class with areEqual, areNotEqual, isTrue, isFalse, isNull, isNotNull.

heading: 10.4 Assertions
text: {{c1::Best practice}}: One logical assertion per test. Test one thing at a time.

heading: 10.5 Test Data and SeeAllData
text: {{c1::Default isolation}}: By default, tests cannot see pre-existing org data. They only see data they create.

heading: 10.5 Test Data and SeeAllData
text: {{c1::@IsTest(SeeAllData=true)}}: Test can see existing org data. Avoid — makes tests fragile and dependent on org state.

heading: 10.5 Test Data and SeeAllData
text: {{c1::Best practice}}: Always create your own test data. Don't depend on org data existing.

heading: 10.6 Testing Triggers
text: {{c1::Insert test records}}: Insert sObjects in your test, then re-query and verify field values reflect trigger logic.

heading: 10.6 Testing Triggers
text: {{c1::Test bulk}}: Insert a list of 200+ records to verify your trigger handles bulk correctly.

heading: 10.6 Testing Triggers
text: {{c1::Test all paths}}: Test before insert, before update, after insert, after update — every branch of your trigger.

heading: 11.1 @future Methods
text: {{c1::What}}: A simple way to run Apex asynchronously. Mark a static method with @future.

heading: 11.1 @future Methods
text: {{c1::Must be static}}: Future methods must be static, return void, and accept only primitive or collection of primitive parameters.

heading: 11.1 @future Methods
text: {{c1::Limits}}: 50 future calls per transaction. Async limits are higher than sync (200 SOQL, 12 MB heap, 60s CPU).

heading: 11.1 @future Methods
text: {{c1::Use case}}: Make a web service callout, do work that shouldn't block the user, fire-and-forget logic.

heading: 11.2 Queueable Apex
text: {{c1::What}}: A more powerful async option that supports non-primitive types and job chaining.

heading: 11.2 Queueable Apex
text: {{c1::Interface}}: Implement Queueable and the execute(QueueableContext) method.

heading: 11.2 Queueable Apex
text: {{c1::Chaining}}: Call System.enqueueJob() at the end of execute() to chain jobs together.

heading: 11.2 Queueable Apex
text: {{c1::Limits}}: 1 queueable job per transaction (50 with chaining via TransactionFinalizers).

heading: 11.3 Batch Apex
text: {{c1::What}}: Process millions of records in chunks. Each chunk runs in its own transaction with its own governor limits.

heading: 11.3 Batch Apex
text: {{c1::Interface}}: Implement Database.Batchable<SObject> with three methods: start, execute, finish.

heading: 11.3 Batch Apex
text: {{c1::Database.Stateful}}: Implement to maintain state (instance variables) across execute() calls. Default is stateless.

heading: 11.3 Batch Apex
text: {{c1::Batch size}}: Up to 2000 per chunk (default 200). Larger chunks may hit heap limits.

heading: 11.4 Scheduled Apex
text: {{c1::What}}: Run Apex at a specific time. Cron-like scheduling.

heading: 11.4 Scheduled Apex
text: {{c1::Interface}}: Implement Schedulable and the execute(SchedulableContext) method.

heading: 11.4 Scheduled Apex
text: {{c1::Schedule expression}}: Cron syntax: 'Seconds Minutes Hours DayOfMonth Month DayOfWeek Year'. Use System.schedule().

heading: 12.1 The ApexDoc Block
text: {{c1::What it looks like}}: /** Description followed by tags. */ placed immediately above classes, methods, or properties.

heading: 12.1 The ApexDoc Block
text: {{c1::@param}}: Describes a method parameter. One per parameter.

heading: 12.1 The ApexDoc Block
text: {{c1::@return}}: Describes what the method returns.

heading: 12.1 The ApexDoc Block
text: {{c1::@throws}}: Documents the exception types a method can throw.

heading: 12.1 The ApexDoc Block
text: {{c1::@author, @since, @see, @deprecated}}: Additional descriptive tags.

heading: 12.2 Why Document?
text: Why Document?: {{c1::AI agents and code-completion tools use doc comments to understand intent.}}

heading: 12.2 Why Document?
text: Why Document?: {{c1::Future developers (including future you) will thank you.}}

heading: 12.2 Why Document?
text: Why Document?: {{c1::Auto-generated documentation tools can produce reference docs from your comments.}}

heading: 12.2 Why Document?
text: Why Document?: {{c1::ApexDoc tags are required by Salesforce for managed packages and AppExchange apps.}}
